coredata.dc:description: Improper pesticide use induces oxidative stress and disrupts detoxification systems in plants. We synthesized CDs with cascading nanozyme activities to mitigate phytotoxicity in lettuces under imidacloprid (IMI) stress. CDs exhibit superoxide dismutase-like (SOD-like) and peroxidase-like (POD-like) activities. Surface modifications and analysis of CDs, the SOD-like activity relies on the -NH2, -COOH, and -OH groups for binding superoxide anions (O2 •-), while POD-like activity depends on -COOH and CO groups, also, CO group provides π-system and the electron-deficient structure for electron transfer. Practically, under IMI stress, CDs strengthen multiple defense systems in lettuces, reducing levels of reactive oxygen toxicity (O2 •-, H2O2, and MDA, by 26.77 %, 48.52 %, and 13.10 %, respectively). Meanwhile, CDs upregulate detoxification gene expression, resulting in a 42.74 % reduction in IMI residue in lettuces. Moreover, the acceptable daily intake of IMI in lettuces treated with CDs was less than 18.0 % of the reference dose, even at high-concentration IMI.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of CDs with cascading nanozyme activity for scavenging free radicals, reducing cell toxicity, and decreasing IMI residue in lettuce. (A) Preparation of CDs and their mechanism of scavenging free radicals. (B) Corresponding mechanisms of CDs reducing ROS and IMI residue in lettuces. The picture was drawn by Figdraw (www.figdraw.com).

The characterization of the CDs. (A) The TEM image of CDs. Inset of the panel (A): size distribution of CDs. (B) The UV–vis absorption (Abs), excitation, and emission spectrum of CDs. (C) The FT-IR spectrum of CDs. (DF) The High-resolution C 1 s, N 1 s, and O 1 s spectra of CDs, respectively.

Surface modification of CDs for structure-property relationship investigation. (A) Illustration modifications of amino, carboxyl, hydroxyl, and carbonyl groups on the surface of CDs. (B) SOD-like activity change of 100 μg/mL CDs before and after modifications. (CF) High-resolution C 1 s, (G-J) N 1 s, and (KN) O 1 s spectra of CDs(C=O), CDs(-COOH/C=O), CDs(-COOH/-OH/C=O), and CDs(-COOH/-OH/-NH2), respectively. In (B), P values are determined with one-way ANOVA Tukey's multiple comparisons test. Data are presented as means ± SE from three independent experiments.

Evaluation of antioxidant systems in lettuce plants treated with different concentrations of GABA and CDs under IMI stress. (A-G) Antioxidant oxidase activity (SOD, CAT, POD, APX, MDHAR, DHAR, and GR). (HI) The content of AsA and DHA. CK: H2O. IMI: IMI stress, the application concentration of IMI was 2 g/L. IG1: IMI + GABA (200 μg/mL). IG2: IMI + GABA (300 μg/mL). ICDs1:IMI + CDs (25 μg/mL). ICDs2: IMI + CDs (50 μg/mL). ICD3: IMI + CDs (100 μg/mL). Data are the means of three replicates with SE, and different letters indicate a significant difference (P < 0.05).

The pathway of phenylpropanoid biosynthesis. The box diagram shows lignin content, and the heat map shows gene expression. CK: H2O, I: IMI, IC: IMI + CDs. Data are the means of three replicates with SE, and different letters indicate a significant difference (P < 0.05).

CDs activated signaling networks in lettuces under IMI stress. I: IMI, IC: IMI + CDs.The heat maps indicate gene expression, the red indicates upregulated, and the blue indicates down-regulated. Additionally, the red fonts indicate significant up-regulation, green fonts indicate significant down-regulation and black fonts indicate no significant difference or not detected. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

The growth of lettuce and dissipation dynamics of IMI in lettuce. (A) Photo of lettuce plants treated with water (H2O) and CDs for 15 days after imidacloprid stress. (B) IMI degradation and IMI content (inset). (C) Tseudo-first-order fitting for IMI degradation.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Improper pesticide use induces oxidative stress and disrupts detoxification systems in plants. We synthesized CDs with cascading nanozyme activities to mitigate phytotoxicity in lettuces under imidacloprid (IMI) stress. CDs exhibit superoxide dismutase-like (SOD-like) and peroxidase-like (POD-like) activities. Surface modifications and analysis of CDs, the SOD-like activity relies on the -NH2, -COOH, and -OH groups for binding superoxide anions (O2 •-), while POD-like activity depends on -COOH and CO groups, also, CO group provides π-system and the electron-deficient structure for electron transfer. Practically, under IMI stress, CDs strengthen multiple defense systems in lettuces, reducing levels of reactive oxygen toxicity (O2 •-, H2O2, and MDA, by 26.77 %, 48.52 %, and 13.10 %, respectively). Meanwhile, CDs upregulate detoxification gene expression, resulting in a 42.74 % reduction in IMI residue in lettuces. Moreover, the acceptable daily intake of IMI in lettuces treated with CDs was less than 18.0 % of the reference dose, even at high-concentration IMI.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that support the findings of this study are available in the supplementary material of this article.

ce:section.ce:para: Lettuces (Lactuca sativa) are widely cultivated as a leafy vegetable with significant economic value (Kim, Moon, Tou, Mou, & Waterland, 2016). However, they are vulnerable to various insect pests, such as aphids, Agrotis ipsilon, and whiteflies, which not only reduce the yield and quality of the lettuce by feeding on leaves but also transmit diseases (Del Pozo-Valdivia, Morgan, & Bennett, 2021; Feng, Chen, & Ying, 2004; Sulvai, Chaúque, & Macuvele, 2016). Conventional agricultural practices commonly address insect infestations by frequently applying pesticides, particularly insecticides, to the foliage. Among these pesticides, imidacloprid (IMI), the second most widely used insecticide globally, is highly effective against sucking insects and some chewing pests affecting vegetables like solanaceous crops, leafy greens, and melons (Cámara, Cermeño, Martínez, & Oliva, 2020; Li et al., 2018; Liu et al., 2021; Shakir, Irfan, Akhtar, & S.u. Rehman, M.K. Daud, N. Taimur, A. Azizullah., 2018; Sharma et al., 2021). Despite its efficacy, only about 0.3 % of these pesticides are absorbed by target pests, leaving most to accumulate in the environment and be absorbed by plants (Shan et al., 2022). To maintain normal growth, plants rely on detoxification mechanisms to metabolize these pesticides; excess pesticides often evade complete degradation (Liang et al., 2022; Zhu et al., 2022). They residues persist in plant tissues, posing a threat to food safety, especially in leafy vegetables, and increasing the risk of human cancer (Sharma et al., 2021). Research conducted by Hillary et al (Craddock, Huang, Turner, Quirós-Alcalá, & Payne-Sturges, 2019). examined approximately 650,000 samples and found that IMI was the most prevalent in vegetables, including 45.6 % of lettuce samples. Therefore, reducing pesticide residues in vegetable crops, particularly in lettuces, has become a critical concern.

Beyond pesticide residues, the excessive utilization of IMI also triggers cell toxicity due to generating reactive oxygen species (ROS), malondialdehyde (MDA), DNA lesions, etc., creating a new form of stress (Shakir et al., 2018). Typically, plants respond by activating antioxidant defense systems, including enzymes like superoxide dismutase (SOD), peroxidase (POD), catalase (CAT), along with the ascorbate-glutathione (AsA-GSH) cycle, as well as enhanced secondary metabolites, and signaling molecules like plant hormones and calcium to mitigate ROS effects (Cheng et al., 2024; Gill & Tuteja, 2010; Zhu, Chen, Shi, & Zhang, 2016). However, plants' inherent self-regulation mechanisms are often inadequate for managing severe external stressors, leading to oxidative stress and subsequent declines in growth, development, and biosynthetic activities (Dong et al., 2023a; Li et al., 2018; Liu, Li, et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2021). Enhancing the ability of plants to scavenge ROS is crucial for improving pesticide resistance and promoting plant growth. Current strategies to regulate pesticide stress involve the addition of free radical scavengers or growth regulators such as γ-aminobutyric acid (Shan et al., 2022), melatonin (Liu, Li, et al., 2021), and jasmonic acid (Yu et al., 2022) to facilitate intracellular ROS scavenging and reduce oxidative damage. However, efforts to mitigate pesticide-induced stress remain in the early stages, and there is still a need to further explore free radical scavengers with strong effectiveness and low toxicity to solve the stress damage caused by applying various pesticides.

Carbon dots (CDs) have emerged as a promising class of carbon-based nanomaterials due to their small size and excellent biocompatibility (Huang et al., 2023; Wang et al., 2022; Xue, Fu, Dong, Yang, & Zhou, 2020). These materials possess abundant functional groups and unpaired electrons, enabling them to act as antioxidant nanozaymes for scavenging ROS (Ren et al., 2022; Sun, Zhou, Ren, & Qu, 2018). Nanozymes, are a type of nanomaterial that exhibits enzyme-like activity, such as mimicking the functions of SOD, CAT, POD, and ascorbate peroxidase (APX), among others (Sun et al., 2018; Wu et al., 2023; Yana et al., 2023). Compared to natural enzymes, nanozymes offer enhanced physical and chemical stability, greater durability, and lower costs when exposed to harsh environments. To date, CDs nanozymes have been successfully used to mitigate ROS production caused by salt stress (Jiawen et al., 2023) and drought stress (Li et al., 2020) in plants, although their underlying mechanisms remain unclear. Furthermore, CDs have alleviated the stress caused by the herbicide 2,4-dichlorophenoxyacetic acid in rice cells (Li et al., 2020). Yet, the mechanisms behind this protective effect are still not completely understood. Transcriptomic studies have proven valuable in revealing the role of CDs in regulating gene expression in response to various stresses (Guo et al., 2024; Zhang et al., 2023). However, there are few reports of applying CDs to resist insecticide stress and reduce plant residues, let alone their potential gene regulatory effects.

To address the challenges above, we synthesized water-soluble CDs using citric acid (CA) and γ-aminobutyric acid (GABA). These CDs exhibited remarkable cascade nanozyme (SOD-like and POD-like) activities, effectively scavenging ROS both in vitro and in lettuce plants, alleviating IMI stress, and reducing residual levels in the lettuces. Firstly, through surface modifications, we elucidated the mechanism underlying the cascade catalysis of CDs: the -NH2, -COOH, and -OH groups for binding O2 •-, the -C=O of CDs provided electron-deficient structure and π-system for electron transfer to produce O2 and H2O2, exhibiting SOD-like activity and restore the structure of CDs to their original state. Then, the -COOH and -C=O of CDs provided POD-like binding sites and catalytically active sites, respectively, to scavenge H2O2 produced in the previous stage to achieve the cascade catalysis of CDs nanozyme ( Fig. 1A ). Secondly, we systematically investigated the regulatory mechanism of CDs nanozymes on lettuce under IMI stress using transcriptomics integrated with biochemical analysis ( Fig. 1B). This study demonstrates CDs nanoenzymes as multifunctional agents capable of amplifying plant signals, scavenging ROS, and degrading pesticides, all while enhancing food safety for future agricultural practices.

The imidacloprid (IMI) is a commercial pesticide with a 5 % active ingredient, and the recommended concentration is 1 g/L. The citric acid (CA), γ-aminobutyric acid (GABA), 1,3-propane sultone (PS), 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine dihydrochloride (TMB), 1, 4-dioxane, sodium borohydride (NaBH4), 4-sulfophenyl isothiocyanate sodium salt (SPI), and IMI standard, acetonitrile (HPLC) were purchased from Macklin biochemical technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Superoxide dismutase (SOD) and horseradish peroxidase (HRP) were purchased from Shanghai Yuanye Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China).

The CDs were prepared using a one-pot hydrothermal method using GABA and CA. Briefly, GABA (2 g) and CA (0.5 g) were dissolved into ultrapure water (20 mL) by sonication (Ultrasonic machine, 40 kHz, 240 W, Skymen, China) for 30 min. Then, the transparent solution was transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C in an oven (universal oven UN 55, Memmert, Germany) for 6 h. After naturally cooling down to room temperature, the obtained brown solution was filtered through a 0.22 μm filter membrane and dialyzed (MW 500) against ultrapure water for 12 h (refreshing the water every two hours). Finally, the CDs were obtained after vacuum freeze-drying using a vacuum freeze dryer (LGJ-10 ordinary type 0.12 m2, Beijing Songyuan Huaxing Technology Development Co., Ltd., China).

We referred to the previous methodology established by our group to carry out the characterization of CDs (Cen et al., 2023; Huang et al., 2023). Specifically, the morphology of CDs was observed using a JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope (TEM). A Thermo Fisher K-Alpha X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) and Nicolet IS5 Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrophotometer analyzed the component and surface functional groups of CDs. CDs' absorption and fluorescence spectra were measured with a UV-2550 Shimadzu Spectrophotometer (200–800 nm) and an F-700 Hitachi Fluorescence Spectrophotometer. Finally, the ζ potential of CDs was measured in a Malvern ZetaSizer Nano (ZS90) ζ potential analyzer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nanozyme and free radicals scavenging activities in vitro assay
ce:section.ce:para: Nanozyme (SOD-like, POD-like, and CAT-like) and typical free radicals (DPPH•, •OH, O2 •-) were according to Gao et al (Fucheng, Jiamei, Pengyu, Yufei, & Yanyan, 2023). and Liu et al (Jiawei et al., 2023). to evaluate the nanozyme activities and scavenging of CDs. The specific experimental methods are described in the supplementary materials.
Surface modification of CDs was according to Cui et al (Cui et al., 2023). and Gao et al (Gao et al., 2023). The specific experimental methods are described in the supplementary materials.

The plant growth and cultivation experiments were carried out in the Main Campus Teaching & Research Base of South China Agricultural University. Italian lettuce (Lactuca sativa) was used to study the effects of CDs on plant growth. We manually selected lettuce seeds of uniform size. When the seedlings developed four true leaves, those with similar plant height and leaf area were selected again and placed on cultivating shelves, and the formula for the nutrient solution was the same as that reported in our previous study (Li et al., 2020). To verify the limit of the synthesized CDs on the mitigation of pesticide stress, 2 g/L imidacloprid (IMI) was chosen as the optimum concentration after the preliminary experiment, and IMI was treated three times, once every 2 days, GABA and CDs were sprayed 24 h after the last treatment. Solutions of GABA or CDs at different concentrations (200 and 300 μg/mL, according to Shan et al (Shan et al., 2022),.) or (25, 50, and 100 μg/mL) were sprayed on each leaf of Italian lettuce (2-week-old) twice a day until harvest, and marked as IG1, IG2 and ICDs1, ICDs2, ICDs3, respectively. Three replicates of six seedlings were set for each treatment. The control (CK) was treated with distilled water. The seedlings were harvested after 7 days of treatment. The antioxidant enzymes (SOD, CAT, APX, POD, MDAHR, DHAR, and GR), antioxidant substances (AsA, DHA, and GSH,), superoxide anion radical (O2 •-), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), malondialdehyde (MDA), lignin, amino acids, total phenols, total flavonoids, IMI and elemental (P, K, Mg, Fe, Zn, and Cu) content were measured (Detailed determination methods are in the supplementary materials).

In addition, to evaluate the dissipation dynamics of pesticides in lettuce and risk, IMI (3 g/L) was treated three times, once every two days. The CDs (25 μg/mL) solutions were sprayed on each leaf of Italian lettuce (2-week-old) twice a day. The growth (plant size and leaf development) of lettuces was observed, and the content of IMI was determined on 0, 3rd, 6th, 9th, 12th, and 15th.

The Italian lettuce leaves from the IMI and IMI + CDs groups (IMI + 25 μg/mL CDs) were collected and then sent to Sangon Biotech Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China) for transcriptome analysis. Total RNA was separated via the TRlzol reagent (Invitrogen, Carlsbad, CA). Next, RNA concentration and purity were determined by a NanoDrop 2000 (NanoDrop Technologies), and RNA integrity was assessed by an Agilent 2100 Bioanalyser (Agilent). Subsequently, 1 μg of total RNA was used to generate an RNA-seq transcriptome library by Illumina TruSeq RNA sample preparation kit (Illumina, San Diego, CA). The transcriptome library was sequenced by the Illumina NovaSeq. 6000 platform (2 × 150 bp reads). Five databases were selected to annotate the unigenes, including NR, SwissProt, COG, KEGG, and GO. Differentially expressed genes (DEGs) were defined as genes with adjusted q-value ≤ 0.05 and |(Fold Change| > 1.2. The qRT-PCR analysis used an Applied Biosystems 7500 fast real-time PCR system (Roche; software version 2.0.5) with SYBR Green qPCR SMix (ROX; Roche). The gene-specific primers used are provided in the online Table S2.

Genes information was gained from Ensembl plants. (https://plants.ensembl.org/index.html) and NCBI blast (https://blast.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/). Data on IMI persistence was fitted into first-order dissipation kinetics C = C0 e-kt, where C = concentration after a lapse of time t, C0 = apparent initial concentration, and k = rate constant. In both cases, the first-order equation provided a fit for the data, and the half-life values were calculated based on this. The table and Figure values were calculated as the mean ± SE. The antioxidant enzyme activities and the levels of antioxidant substances, including O2 •-, H2O2, MDA, IMI, lignin, amino acids, total phenols, total flavonoids, and mineral elements, etc. were statistically analyzed using one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA), followed by Duncan's multiple range test to determine significance. A threshold of P ≤ 0.05 was set to indicate statistical significance. The analyses were performed using SPSS 18.0 (IBM, USA), and all figures were generated with Origin 2019b (MicroCal Software, Northampton, MA, USA) and Tbtools.

CDs with blue fluorescence emission were synthesized via a one-pot hydrothermal method using citric acid (CA) and γ-aminobutyric acid (GABA) at 180 °C for 6 h. As shown in Fig. 2A, the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image revealed that CDs were typical microspherical particles with an average diameter of about 4.24 nm. The zeta potential was −7.33 mV (Fig. S1A). The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image showed noticeable lattice fringes with an interplanar spacing of 0.21 nm (Fig. S1B), which matched the (100) facet of graphite (Zhu et al., 2023). Moreover, the CDs had absorption bands at 310–330 nm, attributed to the n-π* transition of the CO ( Fig. 2B) (Jian et al., 2017). The photoluminescence (PL) emission peak was located at 455 nm with an excitation wavelength of 326 nm in water ( Fig. 2B).

Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were used to analyze the chemical compositions of CDs. As shown in Fig. 2C, broad absorption bands at 3450 cm−1 and 3340 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibration of -OH and -NH2 groups, respectively (Li et al., 2022). The characteristic peak at 1620 cm−1 could be ascribed to the CO stretching (Zhang et al., 2023). It could also be noted that the asymmetric stretching and bending vibrations of OH were at 1360 cm−1 (Sadhanala et al., 2022; Zhang, Zhang, Chen, et al., 2023). These findings demonstrated the presence of various -OH, -COOH, and -NH2 group configurations on the CDs' surface. XPS analysis further confirmed the surface structure of the CDs, revealing elemental compositions of C (64.53 %), N (7.18 %), and O (28.50 %) (Fig. S1C). C 1 s peak was resolved into three peaks: 284.8 eV (CC), 286.3 eV (C-OH), and 288.3 eV (C=O) ( Fig. 2D) (Thara, Korah, Mathew, John, & Mathew, 2023). N 1 s peak comprises three peaks corresponding to 400.0 eV (C-NH2) and 401.5 (graphitic N) ( Fig. 2E) (Cen et al., 2023). O 1 s peak was resolved into two peaks: 532.2e V (C=O) and 533.5 eV (CO) ( Fig. 2F) (Cui et al., 2023). Based on the XPS and FTIR analysis, the surface of the prepared CDs tended to be composed of functional groups, including -OH, -COOH, -NH2, and CO.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Radicals scavenging and multi-enzymatic properties of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The functional groups on CDs confer strong free radical scavenging abilities (Cui et al., 2023; Gao et al., 2023; Li et al., 2022). The scavenging activity of CDs was investigated using various model radicals such as 2,2-Diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH), hydroxyl radical (•OH), and superoxide anion (O2 •-).

DPPH, characterized by unpaired nitrogen atom structures, could compete with cells for electrons and attack cell membranes (Ionita, 2021). The reduction of DPPH radicals is indicated by the fading of its characteristic purple color. Fig. S2A depicted this phenomenon, showing decreased DPPH elimination with increasing CDs concentration. At a concentration of 100 μg/mL, CDs exhibited an elimination efficiency of approximately 80 %, surpassing many previously reported CDs (Li, Li, Yang, et al., 2020; Roy, Ezati, & Rhim, 2021; Xiao et al., 2024), highlighting the impressive DPPH clearance capability of the CDs used in this study.

•OH, a highly oxidized free radical, can cause significant damage to cell membranes and DNA. By utilizing the Fe2+/H2O2 Fenton reaction (Junjie, Guoyong, Yuqi, Jinghan, & Fuwei, 2023), we observed that CDs competed with salicylic acid (SA) to react with •OH. This competition resulted in a gradual fading of color and increased elimination efficiency of •OH. At a 100 μg/mL concentration, the elimination efficiency of •OH reached approximately 70 % (Fig. S2B), surpassing the reported efficiencies in some literature sources (Chen et al., 2021; Dong, Ma, Huang, Zhang, & Gao, 2022).

Furthermore, superoxides are among the most abundant and highly destructive ROS. Among these, O2 •- is a highly oxidizing superoxide that causes severe oxygen toxicity in organisms, but it can be dismutated by superoxide dismutase enzyme (SOD) (Li, Li, Yang, et al., 2020). Therefore, the elimination rate of O2 •- is also used to indicate SOD enzyme activity. Nitro-blue tetrazolium (NBT) serves as a sensitive indicator for superoxide radicals, forming blue formazan compounds upon reduction. The color intensity of the formazan compound could be used to evaluate the removal efficiency of O2 •- and evaluate SOD enzyme activity (Wang et al., 2022). As shown in Fig. S2C, increasing concentrations of CDs resulted in a gradual fading of the solution's color, indicating the dismutation of O2 •- by the CDs and exhibiting their SOD-like activity. The elimination efficiency of O2 •- was approximately 70 % at a concentration of 75 μg/mL, comparable to that of commercial SOD. Remarkably, the SOD-like activity of the CDs reaches up to 90 % at a concentration of 100 μg/mL, surpassing that of commercial SOD by 1.25 times. To assess the stability of the CDs nanoenzyme, their SOD-like activity was measured under different temperatures and pH values. The results indicate that the CDs retained their activities between 20 °C and 150 °C, highlighting their excellent thermal stability compared to commercial SOD, which rapidly deactivated at 80 °C (Fig. S2D). Additionally, over a wide range of pH values (1–7), CDs maintained high SOD-like activity (Fig. S2E). These further highlighting their potential as an alternative to commercial SOD due to their stability and superior enzymatic activity.

Generally, CDs exhibit multi-enzyme-like properties, including POD, catalase (CAT), SOD, etc (Kong et al., 2022; Muhammad et al., 2022). SOD served as the primary defense mechanism against oxidative stress by converting O2 •- into oxygen and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2). Subsequently, CAT and POD enzymes decompose excess H2O2 into water (H2O) and •OH, respectively. To assess the POD-like activity of the CDs, the decomposition of H2O2 was monitored, and the oxidation of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethyl-benzidine (TMB) in the presence of H2O2 was measured to quantify POD-like activity. As shown in Fig. S2F-G, a deep blue solution was observed when H2O2 and TMB were mixed with CDs, indicating POD-like activity. Additionally, although the enzyme's activity remains stable between 20 and 150 °C, it is lower than that of commercial horseradish peroxidase (HRP) and shows greater sensitivity to changes in pH. Conversely, no significant CAT-like activities were detected in the CDs.

Overall, the CDs exhibited the ability to catalyze the conversion of O2 •- to H2O2 through their superior SOD-like activity. Subsequently, the H2O2 was catalyzed by the CDs with POD-like activity to generate •OH. Additionally, the CDs acted as scavengers for •OH and DPPH•, reducing the levels of •OH and DPPH radicals. These findings illustrate the multifunctional enzyme-like properties of CDs, highlighting their potential for applications in antioxidant and enzyme mimetic systems.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The mechanism of Nanoenzyme Cascade catalysis of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Considering the high SOD-like activity of the prepared CDs, which were functionalized with groups such as -COOH, -OH, -NH2, and CO, we conducted a sequence of surface modifications to explore how the structural features of CDs influence their SOD-like activity and radical scavenging, aiming to elucidate the catalytic mechanism in detail.

To investigate the role of the -COOH, -OH, and -NH2 groups on the surface of CDs in SOD-like activity, we selectively used 1, 3-propanesultone (PS) to deactivate the -COOH, -OH, and -NH2 group (Ali et al., 2004; Liu et al., 2018). PS could react with -COOH, -OH, and -NH2 groups, producing ester, ether, and secondary amine, respectively (Liu et al., 2021). Under alkaline conditions, the ester can hydrolyze back to -COOH, while ether and secondary amine are stable ( Fig. 3A ). As shown in Fig. S3A, the FT-IR spectrum of CDs(C=O), reveals a shift in the CO stretching vibration shifted from 1620 to 1680 cm−1, indicating the formation of an ester. The CO stretching vibration of CDs(-COOH/C=O) was restored to 1620 cm−1, demonstrating the restoration of -COOH groups (Fig. S3B). In addition, the XPS analysis of CDs(C=O) and CDs(-COOH/C=O) shows a reduction in the content of CO, C-OH, -OH, and -NH2, compared with that of CDs ( Fig. 3 , CD, J-H, and K-L). Thus, these results indicate the successful modifications of the surface of CDs(C=O) and CDs(-COOH/C=O). Compared with CDs, the SOD-like of CDs(C=O) and CDs(-COOH/C=O) significantly decreased by 33.92 % and 29.27 %, indicating that the -COOH only contributed 4.65 % catalytic activity of CDs ( Fig. 3B).

To passivate the -NH2 groups on the surface of CDs, we used 4-sulfophenyl isothiocyanate sodium salt (SPI), which reacts with amines to produce an isothiourea linkage ( Fig. 3A) (Pan, Sun, Zhang, Jiang, & Lin, 2016). In the FT-IR spectrum of CDs(-COOH/-OH/C=O), the NH stretching vibration at 3340 cm−1 disappeared while two new peaks appeared at 1080 and 1220 cm−1, according to the characteristic of the sulphonic acid sodium salt and symmetric O=S=O stretching vibration, respectively. Moreover, the peaks range from 600 to 1000 cm−1, attributed to the aromatic nucleus's increased out-of-plane bending vibration (Fig. S3C). The XPS analysis of CDs(-COOH/-OH/C=O) showed that the characteristic peak of -NH2 was significantly reduced, and the content was only 0.23 % ( Fig. 3I). Thus, the CDs and SPI were successful couplings. Compared with CDs, the SOD-like of CDs(-COOH/-OH/C=O) was 83.47 % of that of CDs, indicating that -NH2 contributed 16.53 % of the catalytic activity of CDs. Compared with CDs(-COOH/C=O), -OH contributed 12.74 % of the catalytic activity of CDs ( Fig. 3B).

Finally, we utilized sodium borohydride (NaBH4) to reduce the CO groups on the surface of CDs ( Fig. 3A). As shown in Fig. S3D, the FT-IR spectrum of CDs(-COOH/-OH/-NH2) indicated a decrease in the stretching of CO at 1620 cm−1 was decreased while a new peak of CO appeared at 1090 cm−1. The XPS analysis of CDs(-COOH/-OH/-NH 2 ) showed that the content of CO was reduced while the content of C-OH increased. Thus, CDs(-COOH/-OH/-NH2) with passivate CO groups were obtained ( Fig. 3F). The SOD-like of CDs(-COOH/-OH/-NH2) was 54.04 % of that of CDs, indicating that CO contributed 45.96 % of the catalytic activity of CDs ( Fig. 3B).

The catalytic mechanism of the POD-like activity of carbon-based nanozymes was reported (Kong et al., 2022; Xu et al., 2022). The -COOH groups serve as the binding sites for H2O2, and CO groups act as the catalytically active sites for converting H2O2 to ROS (Sun et al., 2015). For our CDs, both -COOH and CO could be found, which enhances the POD-like activity. Moreover, the elimination of DPPH, •OH, and O2 •- of CDs(C=O), CDs(-COOH/C=O), CDs(-COOH/-OH/C=O), and CDs(-COOH/-OH/-NH2) was significantly inhibited, and the CDs(-COOH/-OH/-NH2) were the lowest (Fig. S4AC). Additionally, between 20 °C and 150 °C, the SOD enzyme of these CDs remained relatively stable, again indicating the stability of CDs-based nanozymes (Fig. S4D). Together, the presence of CO, -OH, -COOH, and -NH2 groups on the surface of CDs is closely related to their nanozyme and radical scavenging activities, with CO groups identified as the most catalytically active sites.

Considering the conjugated framework of CDs with unpaired electrons, according to reports by Gao et al (Gao et al., 2023). and Cui et al ( Cui et al., 2023
),. we propose a possible catalytic mechanism. The O2 •- is anchored to the surface of the CDs via the hydrogen bonding with the -COOH, -OH, and -NH2 groups. Then, the electron-deficient structure on the CDs accepts an electron from O2 •- to generate O2 and reduced-state CDs. Due to the large π-system (C=O) on the CDs, a p-π conjugation with the obtained electron forms, stabilizing the intermediate product (reduce-state CDs). Subsequently, another superoxide gets an electron from the reduce-state CDs, resulting in the production of H2O2, while the structure of CDs returns to their original ones. Then, the -COOH and CO groups of CDs then serve as POD-like binding sites and catalytically active sites, respectively, to scavenge H2O2 produced in the previous stage, thus enabling the cascade catalysis of the CDs nanozyme.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs enhance the antioxidant system to reduce toxicity in lettuce cells
ce:section.ce:para: Low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility make CDs widely used in agricultural planting. The cytotoxicity of CDs was assessed using the CCK8 assay to measure their impact on the proliferation and apoptosis of HeLa cells. As shown in Fig. S5, CDs exhibited low cytotoxicity and high biocompatibility even at elevated concentrations (100 μg/mL). Considering the excellent ability of the prepared CDs to scavenge ROS and their low cytotoxicity, we investigated their capacity to mitigate IMI-induced stress in lettuce plants. IMI, a commonly used pesticide, can accumulate in vegetables when over-applied, resulting in high levels of ROS and malondialdehyde (MDA). These increases can damage cell membranes and DNA, ultimately inhibiting plant growth (Dong et al., 2023b; Zhang et al., 2021). In our study, the damage caused by IMI stress (the concentration applied was 2 g/L, twice the recommended dose) on lettuce plants was characterized by an increase in ROS (O2 •- by 15.49 % and H2O2 by 5.86 %), lipid peroxidation (MDA by 25.57 %), and inhibition of plant growth (Fig. S6, A-C, and S7). This damage was likely associated with the high absorption of IMI by the plants (Fig. S6D). Although GABA has previously shown efficacy in reducing pesticide toxicity in tomatoes (Shan et al., 2022), current results demonstrate that treatment with ICDs1 (IMI +25 μg/mL CDs) significantly reduced the IMI content by 42.74 %, which was higher compared to other treatments such as IG1 (IMI + 200 μg/mL GABA, reduction of 5.47 %), IG2 (IMI + 300 μg/mL GABA, reduction of 18.50 %), ICDs2 (IMI + 50 μg/mL CDs, reduction of 2.49 %) and ICDs3 (IMI + 100 μg/mL CDs, reduction of 24.49 %) (Fig. S6D).

To alleviate the damage caused by IMI stress, plants activate their antioxidant systems to counteract the damage caused by ROS and lipid peroxidation. The SOD, POD, and CAT are the main antioxidant enzymes, which catalyze the O2 •- and H2O2 to less harmful daughter products, thereby reducing MDA content to protect cells (Gill & Tuteja, 2010). Compared to the effects of GABA in alleviating IMI stress, lettuce plants treated with CDs exhibited significantly higher antioxidant enzyme activity, with substantial reductions in ROS and IMI content. Specifically, ICDs2 treatment led to a notable 22.09 % increase in SOD activity compared to IMI stress alone, showing a superior effect over other treatments ( Fig. 4A ). Moreover, ICDs1 treatment significantly increased CAT and POD activities by 57.35 % and 44.04 %, respectively, which were higher than other treatments ( Fig. 4 , BC). Additionally, ICDs1 treatment significantly reduced the levels of O2 •-, H2O2, and MDA by 26.77 %, 48.52 %, and 13.10 %, respectively (Fig. S6, A-C), demonstrating its effectiveness in alleviating oxidative stress and lipid peroxidation induced by IMI stress.

The ascorbate-glutathione (AsA-GSH) cycle is a vital antioxidant pathway in plants, allowing them to withstand environmental stress by managing ROS and maintaining redox homeostasis (Kaya, Ashraf, Alyemeni, Corpas, & Ahmad, 2020). Key enzymes in the AsA-GSH cycle include glutathione reductase (GR), monodehydroascorbate reductase (MDHAR), dehydroascorbate reductase (DHAR), and ascorbate peroxidase (APX) (Liu, Li, et al., 2021). In our study, ICDs1 treatment in lettuce under IMI stress significantly enhanced the enzyme activities within this cycle, which APX activity increasing by 64.20 %, MDHAR by 61.57 %, DHAR by 52.33 %, and GR by 40.07 % (Fig. 4 , D-G). APX and GR are enzymes that maintain cellular GSH and AsA levels (Zhang & Yang, 2021). The ICDs1 treatment enhanced the activities of these enzymes, which contributed to the effective scavenging of ROS and maintaining the balance of the AsA-GSH cycle. MDHAR converts monodehydroascorbate (MDHA) to ASA or dehydroascorbate (DHA). DHA can be further transformed into AsA with the help of DHAR and GSH, thus removing excess ROS (Liu, Li, et al., 2021). Our findings revealed that ICDs1 treatment notably enhanced the activities of GR and MDHAR, which led to a 67.25 % increase in AsA levels and a 45.90 % decrease in DHA levels ( Fig. 4 , HI). This shift improved the AsA/DHA ratio, strengthening the AsA-GSH cycle and resulting in a substantial reduction in ROS content under ICDs1 treatment.

Overall, our findings suggest that CDs (specifically ICDs1) could alleviate oxidative stress induced by IMI in lettuce plants by maintaining the activities of key enzymes involved in the AsA-GSH cycle, thereby promoting redox balance and enhancing antioxidant defense mechanisms in the plants. Therefore, we chose 25 μg/mL as the optimal concentration of CDs for the following experiments.

To investigate the molecular-level mechanisms underlying the response of lettuce plants to CDs under IMI conditions, we conducted transcriptome analysis to identify differentially expressed genes (DEGs) between the IMI (I) and IMI + 25 μg/mL CDs (IC) groups. Illumina high-throughput sequencing generated approximately 38–46 million raw reads for each sample, with over 79 % and 86 % clean reads retained in the I and IC samples, respectively (Table S1). All clean reads shared high Q20 (>99 %), Q30 (>96 %), and GC (>46 %) values, respectively, suggesting strict data quality control. Furthermore, the principal component analysis (PCA) diagram (Fig. S8A) showed significant separation between the I and IC groups, indicating a substantial impact of CDs on gene expression. Based on RNA transcriptome sequencing analysis, 3950 DEGs were identified between the I and IC groups. Among these, 1885 DEGs were upregulated, while 2095 DEGs were down-regulated (|fold change| >1.2, qvalue < 0.05) (Fig. S8B). To validate the results, 8 DEGs were randomly selected for qRT-PCR validation (genetic information is shown in Table S2), and the results are consistent with the RNA-seq data, confirming the reliability of the RNA-seq results (Fig. S9).

The DEGs identified in the transcriptome analysis were further analyzed using Gene Ontology (GO) and Kyoto Encyclopedia of Genes and Genomes (KEGG) functional pathway analysis to gain insights into the molecular mechanisms underlying the response of lettuce plants to CDs under IMI stress. The 3950 DEGs were classified into three main categories: biological processes, cellular components, and molecular functions, with 50 subgroups to elucidate their functions. The results reveal that the DEGs are involved in various processes such as the “metabolic process,” “response to stimulus,” “detoxification,” “signal,” etc. In terms of cellular components, the DEGs were located in “organelle,” “cell,” “cell part,” etc. Additionally, at the molecular level, the DEGs exhibited functions like “signal transducer activity,” “transcription factor activity, “antioxidant activity,” etc. (Fig. S10).

The analysis conducted on DEGs using KEGG enrichment pathways revealed several significant findings. Firstly, the DEGs were associated with pathways related to signaling, indicating that the treatment with CDs induced significant changes in signaling pathways in lettuce plants in response to IMI stress. These pathways mainly included plant hormone signal transduction, MAPK signaling pathway-plant, AMPK signaling pathway, cAMP signaling pathway, and cGMP-PKG signaling pathway (Fig. S11, A, C). Additionally, the up-KEGG enrichment analysis highlighted the involvement of DEGs in phenylpropanoid biosynthesis (Fig. S11A). Phenylpropanoids serve as precursors for various secondary metabolites, such as lignin and flavonoids, which are known to play crucial roles in plant stress resistance. Therefore, CDs treatment may enhance plant stress resistance by modulating phenylpropanoid biosynthesis. On the other hand, the down-KEGG enrichment analysis indicated disturbances in the biosynthesis of amino acids and carbon metabolism (Fig. S11B). This could be attributed to the imbalance in the carbon-to‑nitrogen (C: N) ratio in lettuce caused by CDs treatment (Huang et al., 2022). Further investigation is required to elucidate the specific mechanisms underlying these effects.

In conclusion, the study suggests that CDs treatment regulates signaling pathways and phenylpropanoid biosynthesis to alleviate IMI stress in plants. However, it also impacts specific metabolic processes, such as amino acid biosynthesis and carbon metabolism.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs upregulate detoxification genes to promote lettuce cell detoxification
ce:section.ce:para: In plant cells, pesticide metabolism is typically divided into three phases involving a series of enzyme-catalyzed reactions. In phase I, pesticide molecules undergo oxidation, catalyzed by cytochrome P450 (CYP450), generating oxidized intermediates. In phase II, these oxidation products are subsequently conjugated with GSH or glucose through the action of glutathione S-transferases (GST) or UDP-glucosyltransferases (UGT), resulting in more water-soluble conjugates, which significantly reduce their toxicity. In phase III, these conjugates are transported to the cell vacuole or extracellular environment by membrane-bound ABC transporters, where they undergo further degradation and detoxification. It is noteworthy that CYP450s, GSTs, UGTs, and ABC transporters all belong to multi-gene families, which are widely distributed in plants and play a crucial role in the metabolism and detoxification of xenobiotics (Kebeish, Azab, Peterhaensel, & El-Basheer, 2014; Zhang, Lu, Zhang, Lu, & Yang, 2016). In our study, 112 detoxification genes showed altered expression due to CDs treatment under IMI stress, members of the CYP450, UGT, GST, and ABC families. Among these, 58 DEGs were upregulated and 54 DEGs were downregulated in the “IC vs. I” group, reflecting a substantial response of detoxification pathways to the combined treatment (Fig. S12D).

In our findings, 56 genes encoding CYP450 enzymes were identified, with 29 of these genes showing upregulation (Fig. S12A). These upregulated CYP450 genes are key to promoting pesticide oxidation in Phase I of metabolism. For Phase II, 16 genes coding UGT and 5 genes coding GST were also significantly upregulated (Fig. S12B). These accelerate the binding of oxidation products from the previous stage to GSH or glucose, as GSH content does not significantly increase when the ascorbate-glutathione cycle is enhanced (Fig. S13). In phase III, the detoxification process relies on ABC transporters, particularly those in the ABC G subfamily, which are responsible for compartmentalizing toxicants in organelles like vacuoles (Zhang & Yang, 2021). For example, the PDR1 was responsible for Cd efflux (Cao et al., 2024; Yu et al., 2019). In the “IC vs. I” group, 8 ABC genes were upregulated, with 5 genes (ABCG1, ABCG15, PDR1, PDR1, and ABCG35) belonging to the ABC G subfamily (Fig. S12C) (genes information in Table S4). Notably, compared to IMI alone, 2 LsPR1 (gene LSAT_4X104160 and gene-LSAT_1X33901) were upregulated by 1.21 and 1.5 times, respectively, after CDs treatment, highlighting them as potential key genes for the efflux of IMI residues.

In summary, CDs play a significant role in upregulating detoxification genes, specifically those within the CYP450, UGT, GST families, and the ABC G subfamily, thereby enhancing the metabolism of IMI and reducing its toxicity in lettuce. This upregulation supports the oxidation, conjugation, and sequestration processes essential for the efficient detoxification of IMI residues, ultimately promoting lettuce's resilience against pesticide stress.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs strengthen Phenylpropanoid biosynthesis in lettuces to alleviate IMI stress
ce:section.ce:para:
Phenylpropanoids serve as crucial precursors for the biosynthesis of lignin, flavonoids, and other secondary metabolites that play essential roles in plant stress resistance (Qiao, Zhang, Liu, & Yang, 2023). Previous studies have demonstrated that the downregulation of genes encoding cinnamyl CoA reductase (CCR), cinnamyl alcohol dehydrogenase (CAD), quinate hydroxycinnamoyl transferase (HCT), and peroxidase (PRX) can reduce lignin content and hinder phenylpropanoid biosynthesis under IMI stress, leading to diminished antioxidant capacity in rice (Wu, Ge, Zhu, & Liu, 2023). In the current study, 32 genes involved in the phenylpropanoid biosynthesis pathway were identified, with 26 genes showing up-regulation in response to CDs treatment (genes information in Table S5). These upregulated genes were distributed across multiple branches involved in lignin biosynthesis ( Fig. 5
). Notably, PRX, a key enzyme in lignin synthesis with antioxidant properties, plays a crucial role in scavenging excess H2O2 in plant tissues, cross-linking oxidized poly-lignin molecules within the cell wall, and providing protection against stress (Meng, Fan, & Rasmussen, 2021; Yang et al., 2022). The results indicated that 9 PRX genes were upregulated in response to CDs treatment, resulting in a 12.61 % increase in lignin content compared to IMI stress alone ( Fig. 5 box diagram). This upregulation of PRX genes by CDs treatment enhances lignin biosynthesis, strengthens phenylpropanoid biosynthesis, and improves the plant's ability to scavenge excess H2O2, thereby enhancing lettuce resistance to IMI stress.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs regulate the signal crosstalk network in lettuce to alleviate IMI stress
ce:section.ce:para: In response to various abiotic stresses, plant cells couple various stimulus signals to cause a series of molecular response mechanisms for plant defense responses (Demidchik, Maathuis, & Voitsekhovskaja, 2017; Jalmi et al., 2018; Miricescu, Goslin, & Graciet, 2018; Peck & Mittler, 2020; Xiaotong et al., 2023). In our study, depicted in the top 20 up-KEGG enrichment map, DEGs were notably enriched in 7 signaling pathways (Fig. S11A). Analysis of all DEGs, 56 DEGs were enriched in the plant hormone signal transduction pathway (primary signal), while 147 DEGs were enriched in secondary signal pathways. These secondary pathways include mitogen-activated protein kinase (MAPK), phosphatidylinositol 3-hydroxy kinase (PI3K), cyclic adenosine monophosphate (cAMP), calcium (Ca2+), adenosine 5′-monophosphate (AMP)-activated protein kinase (AMPK), cyclic guanosine monophosphate (cGMP), and phospholipase D (PLD) (Fig. S14, A-B). Moreover, these signaling pathways were significantly upregulated by CDs under IMI stress conditions (Fig. S14A). This suggests that CDs may play a role in coordinating and regulating the oxidative stress induced by IMI stress by activating and interconnecting multiple signal channels. The activation of these pathways likely facilitates signal cascade amplification within plant cells, enhancing the coordination and regulation of the oxidative stress response.

In plant cells, hormones initiate signaling cascades that are amplified through intracellular signaling molecules, also known as second messengers. These include molecules like cAMP, cGMP, Ca2+, and phospholipase D (PLD), which play a key role in further propagating the initial hormone signals (Mohanta, Bashir, Hashem, & E.F. Abd_Allah, A.L. Khan, A.S. Al-Harrasi., 2018; Waadt et al., 2022). In this study, 13, 12, 8, and 7 genes associated with these second messengers were positively responsive to CDs under IMI stress conditions (Fig. S14A). This response suggests that CDs facilitate the effective relay from primary hormone signals to secondary messengers, strengthening the signaling cascade. These second messengers are essential in amplifying signals by inducing reversible phosphorylation of proteins, which is tightly controlled by protein kinases and phosphatases. These amplified signals eventually reach the nucleus, where they regulate gene expression and induce cellular responses (Xiaotong et al., 2023). Under IMI conditions in this study, 39, 14, and 13 genes involved in MAPK, PI3K, and AMPK pathways were significantly upregulated by CDs (Fig. S14A), highlighting their role in enhancing the cellular response to stress through these signaling pathways.

Therefore, this upregulation suggests that CDs effectively activate complex signaling networks within lettuce cells under IMI stress conditions, leading to step-by-step signal amplification, ultimately triggering specific cellular responses. These responses may include mechanisms to counter oxidative stress and promote cellular detoxification, highlighting the role of CDs in enhancing plant defense mechanisms against environmental stressors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs improve nutritional quality of lettuce under IMI stress
ce:section.ce:para: The biosynthesis, composition, and concentration of nutrients in green leafy vegetables are influenced by genetics, environmental factors, and growth conditions (Colonna, Rouphael, Barbieri, & De Pascale, 2016; Li et al., 2024). Drought, nanoplastics, and heavy metals exposure can induce oxidative stress, negatively impacting the nutrient profile by reducing biomass, plant pigments, and essential amino acids and causing ion imbalance. Despite these negative impacts, the levels of antioxidant vitamins and secondary metabolites in lettuce have increased, as they are vital for enhancing the plant's defense mechanisms against such stressors (Gao, Xu, Chang, Dong, & Song, 2020; Hefner, Amery, Denaeghel, Loades, & Kristensen, 2024; Lian et al., 2021; Zong et al., 2023). In our study, compared to CK, IMI stress significantly affected the nutritional quality of lettuce by increasing the content of vitamin C (Vc, also known as AsA) ( Fig. 4H) and total phenols (TP), while significantly reducing amino acid content by 9.89 % and total flavonoids (TF) by 13.19 %. Vc and TP serve as important nutrients for lettuce and antioxidants (Sachdev, Ansari, Ansari, Fujita, & Hasanuzzaman, 2021), suggesting that lettuce stimulates its defenses against IMI stress, but is still unable to maintain normal nutrient accumulation. Notably, The application of CDs further enhanced the levels of Vc and TP, resulting in increases of 67.25 % and 7.62 %, respectively, compared to IMI stress. Additionally, CDs treatment improved the accumulation of amino acids and TF under IMI stress, resulting in increases of 20.62 % and 6.79 %, respectively. The amino acid content was particularly significant, surpassing the normal baseline level (8.69 %), suggesting that CDs can enhance nutrient resilience and antioxidant defenses in lettuce under stress conditions. ( Fig. 4H, and Fig. S15A-C).

Furthermore, the toxic effects of IMI on lettuce also influence the absorption and transport of mineral elements. As shown in Fig. S15D-I, IMI stresses increased phosphorus (P) levels while significantly reducing potassium (K) and magnesium (Mg) levels. These elements are primarily involved in regulating the osmotic pressure and maintaining the cell membrane stability (Zeng et al., 2024), which is impaired due to ROS-induced damage. IMI stress also altered the content of the micronutrients, iron (Fe) and copper (Cu) levels increased, whereas the zinc (Zn) levels declined. Fe and Cu are components of antioxidant enzymes (such as SOD and CAT) and participate in cellular redox reactions (Arif et al., 2016; Hendrix, Verbruggen, Cuypers, & Meyer, 2022). Lettuce plants elevate levels of Fe and Cu to cope with IMI stress. The treatment with CDs alleviated ROS toxicity, improved membrane stability, and promoted the accumulation of K and Mg. However, the levels of Fe and Cu were suppressed, and the Zn content exhibited only a slight increase. This may be due to the activation of Ca2+ signaling by CDs, which affects the competitive absorption of Fe, Cu, and Zn (Cherif, Mediouni, Ammar, & Jemal, 2011; Kinraide, Pedler, & Parker, 2004; Zhao, Hamon, Lombi, McLaughlin, & McGrath, 2002). In summary, mitigate the toxic effects of IMI in lettuce and improve its quality.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dissipation dynamics of IMI in lettuce and risk assessment
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the impact of CDs on IMI degradation rate and half-life in lettuce plants, 25 μg/mL CDs were applied to lettuce plants under high concentrations of IMI. The growth of the plants was monitored on the 0, 3rd, 6th, 9th, 12th, and 15th, and their IMI content was measured. Fig. 7A illustrated that compared with the control (CK, H2O), lettuce plants subjected to IMI stress exhibited stunted growth and incomplete leaf expansion. However, the application of CDs significantly alleviated these effects, allowing the plants to return to normal growth patterns.

The degradation rate and kinetics of IMI under different treatments were analyzed, as depicted in Fig. 7B and Table S7. The results showed that the IMI concentration decreased by approximately 70 % and 80 % within 3 and 6 days, respectively, under CDs treatment, which was significantly higher than the CK. These are attributed to the CDs enhancing the antioxidant and detoxification capacity of lettuces to prevent the accumulation and accelerate the degradation of IMI. Furthermore, the degradation rate of IMI continued to increase over time under CDs treatment, reaching over 90 % between 9 days and 15 days, although without a significant difference compared to the CK. Towards the later stages of treatment, the IMI content in lettuces remained relatively stable, possibly due to reduced plant metabolism as they aged. Additionally, both CK and CDs treatments followed first-order kinetics for IMI residue dissipation, with similar half-lives of approximately 0.81 and 0.78 days, respectively, indicating no significant difference in IMI half-life between the two treatments. (p > 0.05) (Fig. 7C and Table S7). Therefore, the study suggests that CDs were effective in promoting the degradation of IMI in lettuce plants, particularly for crops intended for short-term harvest, to ensure pesticide residues are within safe limits.

The risk assessment of IMI in lettuces, based on the European Commission (EC) regulation No. 396/2005, sets the maximum residue level (MRL) for IMI at <0.5 mg/kg. The European Food Safety Authority (EFSA) has reviewed and established this MRL to ensure food safety (Chen et al., 2021b). In the present study, the content of IMI was approximately 0.51 mg/kg in lettuces under IMI condition with CDs treatment, which is close to MRLs, indicating that the CDs treatment effectively reduced IMI residues in lettuce. However, the content of IMI under CK was 0.73 mg/kg, exceeding MRLs (inside Fig. 7B).

To assess the risk associated with IMI residues in lettuces, the acceptable daily intake (ADI) was estimated references Osman et al (Chen et al., 2021c; Osman, Al-Humaid, Al-Rehiayani, & Al-Redhaiman, 2011). and using the equation:
(1)
ADI
ng ·
kg
− 1
·
d
− 1
= C 0 × U I / BW

C0 is the concentration of IMI in lettuce at 6 d under CDs treatment (ng·g−1); UI is the long-term usual intake of lettuce (7.0 g·d−1) (Epa, 2011); BW is the average Chinese body weight (72.5 kg) (General Administration of Sport, 2022). Hence, the estimated ADI values of IMI 49.2 ng·d−1·kg−1. Even if IMI was assumed to be 100 % taken up by the body, the calculated ADI value was significantly lower than (by 18.0 %) the reference dose of 60 ng·d−1·kg−1 (Arora et al., 2008) (Table S8), indicating that the consumption of lettuce sprayed with a high concentration of IMI and treated with CDs for about 6 days does not pose an unacceptable risk to human health.

Overall, the risk assessment results suggest that the application of CDs to reduce IMI residues in lettuce effectively mitigates potential health risks associated with IMI exposure, ensuring that the IMI levels in lettuce remain within safe limits for consumption. This highlights the potential of CDs as a safe and efficient strategy for reducing pesticide residues in food crops and promoting food safety.

In this study, we reported the CDs with cascading nanozymes activities, including superoxide dismutase (SOD) and peroxidase (POD) activities, exhibiting outstanding ROS scavenging capabilities. This unique CDs nanozyme demonstrated exceptional ROS scavenging ability, making it a promising tool for reducing plant toxic substances such as ROS and pesticide residues, ultimately alleviating plants' stress. The -NH2, -COOH, and -OH groups of CDs for binding O2 •-, the electron-deficient structure, and π-system provided by the CO for electron transfer to produce O2 and H2O2, exhibiting SOD-like activity and return the structure of CDs to their original ones. Then, the -COOH and CO of CDs provided POD-like binding sites and catalytically active sites, respectively, to scavenge H2O2 produced in the previous stage, thus achieving the cascade catalysis of CDs nanozyme. Experimental results on lettuce revealed that the CDs nanozymes effectively reduced ROS toxicity induced by imidacloprid (IMI) stress in plant cells. The nanozymes activated multiple defense systems in lettuces, including antioxidant pathways, phenylpropane biosynthesis, and signal cascading and amplification mechanisms. Additionally, CDs nanozymes upregulated detoxification genes, leading to reduced IMI residues in lettuce plants. Risk assessment analyses confirmed that the IMI levels in lettuces treated with CDs were within acceptable daily intake limits, indicating no risk associated with consumption. By utilizing CDs nanozymes to alleviate pesticide stress, manipulate pesticide residues, and ensure food safety, these advanced nanomaterials could play a significant role in enhancing agricultural sustainability and promoting safe food production in the future.

Fengqiong Chen: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Supervision, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Ziyan Zhou: Methodology, Investigation. Na Yang: Investigation. Qin Jiang: Investigation. Xuejie Zhang: Methodology. Haoran Zhang: Methodology. Yinjian Zheng: Investigation. Wei Li: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization.
